sentences.text: Lili-- excuse me-- Lili Springer.
My father's name was Shmuel Aaron Fuchs.
My mother's was Golda Farkus Fuchs.
I was born in Vysk in Czechoslovakia.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was one of seven children, the third one in the family.
Well, my oldest brother's name was Berel.
And my next-- my next sibling's, my sister, was Suri, and then myself, and then Haimi, and Gitu, Piri, and Yitzhak.
We were seven.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a nice house.
My father was a-- a [YIDDISH].
He was a rabbi.
But what he did was-- was all kinds of things.
I always say he used to-- he was an expert in going bankrupt.
Yes.
[LAUGHS] He had some businesses which went bankrupt.
He was a teacher.
He was a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, as a matter of fact, at one point, he was buying apples-- in bloom, mind you, in bloom.
And then, of course, hope that there won't be a storm [LAUGHS] so that the apples were-- will eventually grow.
And then it-- the apples would grow.
And when they were ready to be picked, he'd hire pickers and-- and everything that was needed.
And then he exported it.
Where to, I'm not sure.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at one point, he rented sheep.
Mind you, that's something that nobody has ever heard of.
[LAUGHS] He rented sheep and rented a place for them to-- to graze and a Jewish shepherd, so that he was able to make cheese.
He went up into the mountains.
We were surrounded by mountains, not anywhere near my house.
And he would make cheese.
And I don't they sell-- that they sold anything besides the cheese, and big loaves of cheese.
And that too was sold and exported.
And obviously, he tried all kinds of [LAUGHS] things.
Let's see, what else.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother used to force-- force feed geese, so that we'll have meat.
Not have to buy it.
I mean, those geese.
And then have meat.
And she would render the fat.
That would be the fat for the year.
And we'd sell-- she'd sell the livers.
That was too expensive for us to eat.
As a matter of fact, from what we got for the liver, we were able to buy a new goose and start it all over again.
We tried all kinds of things.
We owned the house.
And we had one field, which my grandfather gave us.
My grandfather, my mother's father, was very wealthy.
And he had a lot of property.
I mean, whatever you can imagine, he had, he had more, just vast properties.
And one of them-- and they were in different towns, too-- and one of the fields was in my town.
And he gave it to us.
I don't know if he signed it over, or we just used it.
And of course, we didn't have the equipment to actually work it.
So we gave it to someone you know, sharecropping.
And in the fall, we'd get whatever they'd grow, which was usually corn and-- and sunflower seeds.
I'm from a small town.
I know all about these things.
We'd own a goat or two.
We usually didn't own a cow.
We didn't have-- it was still a-- excuse me-- it-- it was a town.
It wasn't a village.
So we tried everything you can imagine.
And one of things my grandfather helped us-- help to set him up in business.
That's why I say that he was an expert in going broke.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
And ours, we were very poor.
And it was even more difficult to be poor because my whole family was very well off.
So comparison, you know, I always felt worse.
We didn't go hungry.
We didn't.
But you'd be surprised how hungry you can be if you have bread and not jelly and as much butter as-- as you want.
I mean, you could be actually be hungry, a craving.
So for that I'd have to go to my grandparents.
[SIGHS]
Well, my father was a-- a rabbi.
I mean, he wasn't a rabbi of the town.
But let's put it this way, the young people of the town really were becoming very irreligious.
It was a lot of problem.
And in my family, that wasn't the case.
I mean, we were kind of looked up to because here at this family, the young people don't go away from-- as a matter of fact, my older brother went to yeshiva.
But--
No.
No.
He was sent away for the yeshiva.
Before the yeshiva, he went in town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His friends, who were becoming irreligious, they always wanted to lead him astray, to be able to come back to my father and say, you see, your-- your son also did this or that.
And they could never do it.
And he was friends with them.
And once, [LAUGHS] I'll never forget that, it was already when the Hungarians were there.
You know, we were home till 1944.
And war started in '39.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a friend, a non-Jew, they owned an ice cream parlor.
And they also sold-- sold beer and one of these cafe, maybe you would call it.
And she was a friend of mine.
So I went there on-- on Pessach to my friend.
And I sat down to one of the tables.
It was on Shabbat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, before I got home, my father knew about it.
[LAUGHS] Apparently, one of the boys went to his father, said, Lili was at that ice cream parlor in Tisou.
And they immediately went to my father saying, Lili was seen in the ice cream parlor.
So my-- and then, I'll never forget, because my father was so nice and gave so-- had so much faith in me, he said, if Lili was there, I'm sure she didn't do anything that was wrong.
And he did not accept that.
And when I came home, he told me what was told and what he answered.
And then he said, but it's a better idea for you not to go, because there's [YIDDISH] involved.
Which means that the way things look.
You have to be careful not to make other people stumble.
In other words, they-- they gossiped unnecessarily.
And that in itself is some kind of transgression.
So I almost like caused it.
So that part, he says, it's better idea not to do it.
But thinking back, I always think it was so nice of him to answer without knowing what really happened.
He had faith in me.
And in general, my father was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was angry with my father a great deal because [LAUGHS] I felt he didn't do his job as a head of the family.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] he was very nice to the children all the time.
And he never beat us or anything.
I got a spanking from my father just once in my life--
Because of-- [LAUGHS] he was wrong, that is the only time I was spanked, when he was definitely wrong.
I used to like children.
And you know, my sisters and brothers, I more or less took care of them.
And my sister didn't like children.
So that any time a child was crying said, she said, Lili, it was, Piri is crying.
Lili, Yitzhak.
Everyone knew to call me.
And at one point, they called me, Lili, one of children is crying.
And I said, well, I'm-- I'm getting tired and that-- of doing it all the time.
Let Suri do it.
And I didn't notice that Suri was eating.
I simply didn't notice it.
And my father said, what?
To disturb her in the middle of eating?
And he hit me.
The first [LAUGHS] time, the first and only time.
And I thought he was wrong, because I didn't even know this.
You know, but in general, he was all right with the children.
Especially, he used to like to show off.
With the-- with the youngest was always being taught the sidra of the week, the translation of the sidra.
So to a one-and-a-half year old, being able to say the sidra of the week and the translation, and a few things what it's about was very impressive.
So he used to do that.
[LAUGHS] But he taught us other things too.
Like?
Well, for instance, it was part of our life to say the prayers.
And then [YIDDISH] and It was just part of our life.
And I never really realized that it wasn't part of everybody else's life.
I just figured that everyone learns all those things.
Well, at one point, my father was teaching in a public school, in a religion, because in the public school, everybody had to learn religion.
Jewish children had the class with a Jewish teacher.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he would teach us baruhk and all kinds of things.
And the children didn't know.
And I-- when he asked, how do you say-- what [YIDDISH] do you say for such and such, I'd raise my hand.
[LAUGHS] And he would say, not you.
I got so insulted, [LAUGHS] because he know I knew it, and he wanted to teach the others.
So that, I you used to hold against him.
I couldn't-- I couldn't talk in class, because I was able to show off.
[LAUGHS] And he just simply wasn't interested.
So at one point, he taught.
I told you that he did all kinds of things.
I suppose, in retrospect-- I guess I still have resentment towards him.
But in retrospect, I really shouldn't have as much of resentment as I always did, because for one thing, it was in the 1930s, which was a worldwide recession.
And Morris told many times how-- Who's Morris?
My former husband.
I told you many times how he used to go right here in Chicago with patched pants and-- and stockings, and how he used to ask for a nickel from his mother.
And she said, no, we can't do it.
You know, for ice cream or something.
So things were difficult all over the world.
But of course, at the time, I didn't realize it.
And later on, I didn't think of it.
But actually, it was difficult everywhere.
And I suppose the-- being an expert in going bankrupt might also have had something to do with the fact that it was in the 1930s.
But I suppose he did try.
He-- he was teaching.
He was buying and selling.
And he tried.
[LAUGHS] Well, it was-- I was going to public school.
And it was a Czech public school.
Our town had four different school systems.
We had a Czech school because it was Czechoslovakia, and anybody who wanted to go there, went.
And it turned out to be mostly the Jews, the Jewish children went there, and a few non-Jews.
Whoever elected to go.
And they had the Hungarian school for the Hungarians, which was the population of the town.
And then they had another Hungarian school, which was with the Hungarian Catholics, which was really-- you know what Volkdeutsche means?
We call them Schwabs.
They were originally from Germany, I think, many generations.
But they were still kind of segregated.
And they were Catholics.
So they had a school.
And kind of outside town there were Ukrainians, because the whole area was a Ukrainian area, just our town itself.
No, just neighbors.
I mean, we did have a Jewish neighborhood or something.
Like, we lived in a place where our nearest Jewish neighbor was quite far.
So neighbors.
But those were usually the Protestant Hungarians.
We didn't have anything to do with the Catholics, because they lived in different places, and some of them came to the Czech school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, I was in school regularly at 6:00.
And I went first, second, and third.
And of course, you don't necessarily want to know how brilliant I was.
[LAUGHS] I don't know-- I wasn't brilliant, but I was a good student.
And then for fourth grade, I can't remember exactly what happened.
I told you that my grandparents were quite well off.
And they always liked me.
I used to go there as a child during the summers.
And I think my last aunt got married, and they only had a son left.
They had eight children.
And I think they wanted to have someone-- a child around the house or something.
And my parents, knowing that I will have a much easier [LAUGHS] life there, they encouraged me to go, for just one year.
They really wanted to adopt me.
Yes.
They said, just for one year.
So I went-- [? it ?] was-- this was a village.
And the population, I told you already that the population of the whole area was Ukrainian.
Just isolated towns were Hungarian, like ours and [NON-ENGLISH] was Hungarian.
And then further away there were some Hungarians.
And that was probably from the time it was Austro-Hungary, where they probably planted Hungarians to-- to kind of get a hold of the place, to make it more permanent.
I don't know.
I'm just guessing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I went to my grandparents.
And there, they had the Ukrainian school.
It's a different script.
It's a different language and different everything.
Well, the principal said that I'll have to repeat third grade, because I don't know anything.
Apparently, I accepted it.
School was important to me.
But somehow, I can't remember exactly why and how.
I did accept it.
I suppose I knew that it's really more difficult because I didn't the script, or the-- the-- the-- I couldn't read.
I couldn't write, nor speak.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went to third grade there, and I learned reading and writing.
It was really not too bad.
Well, there, I only had an interaction with the Jewish kids.
And altogether, there were 15 Jewish families there.
That's all.
And with the non-Jewish kids, those were very anti-Semitic.
Not only the kids.
My grandparents told me.
And you-- you would hear from-- from the Ukrainians.
I mean, you didn't hear that in my town.
In my town it was where, let's say, a child would say, dirty Jew.
So the Jewish child would say, you dirty Hungarian.
They'd have a fight.
And we would fight.
You know.
But there it was dangerous.
You couldn't do that.
Well, the Jewish children kept separate.
And I never had a-- a Ukrainian friend.
Since the Jewish community was so small, neighbors also were not Jews.
But I didn't play with them.
I had one friend who lived kind of outside the village.
You know, it was kind of spread-- it wasn't close.
I don't know exactly where she lived.
Very nice girl.
I didn't have much to do with the non-Jews.
My grandparents had-- you know what a kretshme means?
What?
What is that?
Kretshme is kind of a bar.
You know, they drank like fish.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, and the Hungarians too.
[LAUGHS] They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so they'd come to drink.
And then, of course, they'd let go with all the anti-Semitic things.
From time to time, they would-- my-- my grandfather had a very big house.
As a matter of fact, right now, that-- that's the only house that's still standing belonging to Jews.
I was back there, excuse me, in 1991.
All the Jewish houses were-- vandalism, and they're just torn down.
But my grandfather's house was made into the village hall and the movie theater of the village and matnas-- you know, what you call it?
Kind of a-- Warehouse?
No, not a warehouse.
A-- a club.
A village club and a post office, [INTERPOSING VOICES] all in his house, because it was really very, very big.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is what we had all the time.
And he-- he was very well off.
I think this is how they wanted to talk me to staying there.
Because my grandmother said, you know, they knew I didn't want to stay in-- in a small village.
I liked it better in my town.
So my grandmother said, you know, we have some houses in-- in-- in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Kosice, a very big city.
And we are thinking of going there.
You know, and you'll be able to go to school there.
They tried all kinds of this to kind of talk me into it.
I didn't end up doing it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I was there for one year.
And then I came back to-- I just wanted to be in [LAUGHS] my-- my house, I suppose.
It might have been that I felt kind of rejected that they wanted me to-- to go to my grandparents.
I had all kinds of-- of ambivalent feelings, because I felt very flattered that they wanted me, and that they chose me and no one else.
I was flattered and yet really didn't want to.
So I came back home.
And so what do you do?
Go to fourth grade when all my friends have finished fourth grade?
Well, they knew me as a student.
And the principal said, no, he wants me to go to fifth grade.
So I never went to fourth grade, and I never learned long division.
[LAUGHTER] And you'd be surprised for how long-- and I was very good in math.
That was my favorite subject.
[LAUGHS]
For how long the fact that I didn't learn long division made a difference.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went to the fifth grade.
And then, of course, that was it, because that was already-- the war started.
That was already '39.
The war started.
And to the Czech school closed.
I remember, a Shabbat morning, we got up.
Everybody was called-- was-- was recruited to the army, because a war started.
And I think a few days later, they came back, and Czechoslovakia was simply dismembered.
And at first, the Ukrainians took over.
And that was really bad.
They-- it was dangerous for my grandfather, because they said, now we're going to take everything away from you.
Now, you're you're not going to live like you were used to.
And just within a few months, the Hungarians came in.
And that's when things started to be bad really, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, being in a small town, there's no newspaper and certainly not a town radio or something.
There were very few people who had radios.

So the way they used to disseminate news, like all kinds of laws, new laws-- I don't, know maybe in movies you saw, where they'd come to a village-- it was a town, not a village-- to the square with a drum.
Bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, you know, calling everybody together.
And then they'd read off the new laws or anything new.
And then they'd go to different squares like that.
And this how we found out about anything new.
I mean, this is very strange now and here.
But this is how it was.
So it immediately started, where all Jews have to give up their businesses.
No more Jews in business.
And no more Jews can get licenses for like shoemakers, and even artisans.
And each day, there was a different law.
Each day?
Almost.
You know, you-- you felt like it was each day.
It probably wasn't each day.
So they started a new school.
Well, actually, they put us into the Hungarian school.
So we finished the year in a Hungarian school, the regular one.
And then they separated us.
Also in a Hungarian school, but the Jewish children were separate, where the Czech school use to be.
And that's where I-- I finished elementary school.
Through 8th grade.
Yes.
Now in 1941, the Hungarians decided that Jews have to prove their citizenship, that they were-- you know, that they belong there.
Well, my family-- I found out after, I knew that my family came with the Mayflower, [LAUGHS] so to speak, to that town.
They started-- they came in 1709.
From, I don't know.
I read a book.
There's a new book now, which was written by some people in my area, and this is what it said, and I recognize all the names.
And I really don't know.
Maybe from-- I don't know from where.
But my family was the important family.
[LAUGHS] Anyway, they-- they were the first Jews in that town.
So is this it for this tape?
If I-- all right.
Now, like in this book, which I have read just a few years ago, it mentions all the names, which are familiar to me, because they repeat first names.
And of course, last names remain the same.
And that made me realize that we really were there.
In spite of that, you had to prove that you belonged there.
I suppose.
Yes.
I-- I will tell-- tell you how we proved that.
